attributes.stem: A 42-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner (GP) with an eight-month history of difficulty swallowing. He states that the difficulty occurs with both solids and liquids and points to the centre of his chest when asked where the hold up occurs. He states a different GP started him on pantoprazole four months prior and put the presentation down to reflux, but he has had no improvement in symptoms. He noted that he has regurgitated some undigested food over this period when it is particularly bad. He has lost 2 kg over this period and puts it down to not wanting to eat as much as he normally would. He has had no fevers or night sweats. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He is a non-smoker, he rarely consumes alcohol and lives a fit and healthy life. On examination, he appears well and his vital signs are within normal limits. A barium contrast X-Ray is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The stem is describing a patient suffering from achalasia of the cardia, a neuromuscular disorder resulting in a lack of lower oesophageal sphincter relaxation, manifesting clinically with dysphagia to both solids and liquids. Although the symptoms are very similar to those which may be described in oesophageal carcinoma, the patient does not exhibit the common risk factors of increased age, smoking and frequent alcohol consumption. Additionally, he is a fit and healthy individual otherwise with no evidence of B-symptoms of malignancy.

The contrast study image supplied shows the classical features of achalasia of the cardia, with a dilated oesophagus and a 'bird-beaked' tapering of the lower oesophagus down to and including the cardia. There is a fluid level of contrast, confirming stasis and a hold-up of material.

Hiatus hernia is a protrusion of intra-abdominal contents through an enlarged oesophageal hiatus into the thoracic cavity, most commonly, a portion of the stomach. The most common symptoms are heartburn and regurgitation. Early satiety is also a common symptom of a large hiatus hernia as the stomach does not always drain well with a large meal. These hernias are visible on barium swallow X-Ray and show stomach contents within the thoracic cavity.

Carcinoma of the oesophagus is a diagnosis to consider for all causes of dysphagia. The symptoms of this diagnosis often progress from difficulty with solids to difficulty to liquids and may be accompanied by systemic symptoms of fatigue and weight loss. Smoking, alcohol excess, GORD and obesity are the major risk factors of this condition.

Diffuse oesophageal spasm has no specific physical findings but has links with symptoms indistinguishable from cardiac origin pain. However it is often associated with meals and the accompanying dysphagia is intermittent and non-progressive. A barium oesophagram often shows the classic 'corkscrew' oesophagus finding.

Eosinophilic oesophagitis is a chronic immune/allergen mediated resulting in oesophageal epithelial infiltration by eosinophils. It is more commonly seen in younger adults with ever increasing incidence and prevalence. Symptoms most frequently include intermittent dysphagia, chest discomfort and heartburn. Diagnosis requires histopathological analysis with evidence of >15 eosinophils per high-power microscopy field. There are strong links with family history and atopic diseases such as asthma, eczema and allergic rhinitis.
learningPoints: Achalasia is a neuromuscular disorder resulting in lack of relaxation of the lower oesophageal sphincter.
Achalasia is significant for dysphagia to both solids and liquids.
references: O'Neill OM, Johnston BT, Coleman HG. Achalasia: a review of clinical diagnosis, epidemiology, treatment and outcomes. World J Gastroenterol. 2013;19(35):5806-12. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24124325/
